title: Preemption timeout catch swallows the error silently after recycling the worker
description: In tryPreempt(), the catch block after the 2-second race catches the preempt-timeout error, recycles backgroundWorker, but does not log or surface it in any way. If the rejection came from something other than the timeout (e.g. pool.recycle() inside the Promise.race itself throwing), the failure disappears entirely. More importantly, ackResolved stays false but the caller cannot distinguish 'timed out and recycled' from 'rejected for another reason', which is fine only if the finally block always runs correctly — but any secondary throw inside catch would be uncaught.

title: Async drainQueue fires setTimeout without storing the timer handle, preventing cleanup on shutdown
description: In the error-recovery branch of the async drain loop (line 1393), `setTimeout(() => this.drainQueue(), delayMs)` is called with no handle stored, so it cannot be cancelled during scheduler shutdown. If shutdown() is called between the error and the retry timer firing, the timer still fires and calls drainQueue() on a destroyed scheduler. Although `this.destroyed` is checked at the top of drainQueue and returns early, the timer itself leaks for the full delayMs. Across many failures this accumulates; on a long-lived scheduler this is a minor leak, but there is no cancellation on destroy.

title: cancelSession promotion path continues if promotedRecord is undefined, silently orphaning the promoted session
description: When the primary is cancelled and a subscriber is promoted (promotedTo !== undefined), the code checks `promotedRecord !== undefined` inside each if-branch for worker/paused/pending transfer, and also for gate transfer. However, `adjustSessionCount(record, -1)` at line 571 and the `delete promotedRecord.isSubscriber` block at line 573 run unconditionally, but the isSubscriber block also checks `promotedRecord?.isSubscriber`. If promotedRecord is undefined (this.sessions.get(promotedTo) returns undefined), the promotion silently no-ops all transfers, leaving the old primary's worker/pending/gate unreleased and the promoted session in a limbo state. This can only happen if dedupe.cancelSubscriber returns a promotedTo that has no session record, which should not occur normally but is an undefended assumption.

category: retry without backoff cap / wedge risk
title: drainQueue async error path resets drainRetryCount before rejecting the head, allowing infinite retry on the next entry
description: When MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES is exhausted, drainRetryCount is reset to 0 and the failing head is dequeued and rejected. However, if the very next entry in the queue also triggers an error on the next drainQueue invocation, the counter starts from 0 again and the new head gets MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES more attempts before it too is dropped. With a persistently broken pool (e.g. spawn always fails), every queued entry gets MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES*delayMs of retries, not just the first. This is arguably intentional but means a queue of N entries experiencing systematic spawn failure can take O(N * MAX_DRAIN_RETRIES * 2s) before all are rejected, during which pending callers hang.

title: tryPreempt: after a terminal-ack from the victim, pool.acquire() returning null leaves the incoming visible session's acquireSlot hanging forever
description: In the resolvedKind === 'terminal' branch of tryPreempt (lines 905-917), if pool.acquire() returns null, the function returns null. This propagates back to acquireSlot (line 385: `if (preempted !== null) return { workerId: preempted }`), which falls through to the queue path. However, the session was not yet enqueued at that point — the code only reaches the queue path below line 389 if preempted is null AND the function did not throw. So the session DOES get queued correctly. This path is fine; logging an explicit comment about this non-obvious recovery would prevent future regressions.

title: abortAcquisition dispatches decode_cancelled to subscribers with bare catch {}, silently hiding handler exceptions
description: In abortAcquisition(), subscriber handlers are invoked inside `try { h(...) } catch {}`. Exceptions from handlers are silently dropped. While this is the documented pattern throughout the file and handlers 'must not throw', if a handler throws during an abort path, there is no log trace to diagnose it. This is consistent with the rest of the file but worth noting as an opportunity for structured logging. Additionally, if `this.dedupe.subscribers(params.sessionId)` returns a large set, all are notified but none can propagate errors.

title: CoreBudget.release() over-release warning fires only in dev mode, with no production-mode guard on token accumulation
description: If release() is called with a very large cost value (e.g. due to a bug where the same worker is released twice with cost=N), tokens is clamped to capacity by Math.min, but only a dev-mode warning is emitted. In production this is silent. Since the token counter is clamped, there is no integer overflow risk, but duplicate releases silently inflate the available count back to capacity, potentially allowing more workers to be spawned than the budget should permit. A counter of how many times over-release occurred would help diagnose this in production.

title: spawnInner: worker added to pool before wireWorker() runs; if wireWorker throws, worker is in idle set but not wired
description: In spawnInner(), the worker is added to `this.workers` and `this.idle` at lines 471-472, then wireWorker() is called at line 473. If wireWorker() throws (e.g. worker.handle.onError is not a function but something else that throws), the worker is already in the idle set in a partially-initialized state. Subsequent acquire() will return it, but it has no onError/onExit callbacks wired. However since wireWorker() only calls optional methods (.onError?.() and .onExit?.()), in practice it should never throw — this is a low-risk theoretical path.

title: tryPreempt timeout path synthesizes cancel to victimHandlers from a stale snapshot, races with cleanupSession
description: When preemption times out, `victimHandlers` is captured from `this.sessions.get(victimSessionId)?.handlers ?? []` at line 925. This is a live reference to the handlers array. Between the capture and the for..of loop, cleanupSession() is called at line 928, which sets record.handlers but the old array reference is already captured. Then cleanupSession is called again (which is idempotent). The real risk is that the synthesized cancel fires into handlers that may have been partially torn down. The pattern is already used elsewhere with frozen snapshots but this path uses a live reference.

title: drainQueue uses console.error for the only error log in the scheduler; no error taxonomy or structured event
description: The entire scheduler has a single console.error at line 1384 (`[jxl-scheduler] drainQueue error:`) and all other error paths are bare catch{} or silent. There is no structured error taxonomy (error codes, categories), no error event emitted to callers, and no way for monitoring/telemetry to distinguish transient spawn failures from protocol errors. A lightweight onError callback on Scheduler or a structured error type emitted via handlers would allow jxl-session to surface diagnostics without coupling to console.
code_excerpt:
} catch (err: unknown) {
        console.error("[jxl-scheduler] drainQueue error:", err);

title: admissionGate.admit() has no timeout; a stalled gate permanently blocks acquireSlot
description: The admissionGate.admit() call at line 343 is awaited without any timeout. If the AdmissionGate implementation stalls (e.g. a semaphore that is never released due to a bug in the caller), the acquireSlot Promise never resolves or rejects. The AbortSignal only fires if the caller explicitly aborts; a caller that just waits will hang indefinitely. The types.ts comment says 'admit() may resolve after the session was cancelled', implying callers are expected to abort, but there is no enforcement. A configurable admissionTimeoutMs option or a race with the signal would make this more robust.

category: missing null check / potential wrong workerId
title: Dedupe subscriber acquireSlot returns workerId=-1 when primaryRecord has no worker yet
description: When a subscriber dedupes onto a primary session that is still queued (no worker assigned yet), the return value is `{ workerId: primaryRecord?.worker?.id ?? -1 }`. The caller (jxl-session) receives workerId=-1. If jxl-session uses workerId for routing or logging purposes, -1 is a sentinel that may be misinterpreted. The comment in CLAUDE.md contract says 'scheduler.onMessage(sessionId, handler) returns void — no unsubscribe function', but the workerId return from acquireSlot for subscribers is not documented. This is not a crash but a silent ambiguity.

title: spawn() finally block always decrements spawning and removes promise, but if coreBudget.release throws (inside the catch block), spawning is still decremented correctly — however coreBudget null-assertion (!.) may panic at line 445
description: In the catch block of spawn() at line 443, `this.coreBudget!.release(actualCost)` uses a non-null assertion. The outer if at line 415 checks `if (this.coreBudget)` before setting acquiredBudget, so if acquiredBudget is true, coreBudget is non-null. However TypeScript's control flow does not track this across the async boundary in the try/catch, and if a future refactor moves the acquiredBudget flag set, the assertion could become unsound. More practically, if coreBudget.release() itself throws (e.g. capacity invariant violation in dev mode), the throw propagates out of the catch block before the finally runs — but finally always runs regardless, so spawning is correctly decremented. Low risk but the non-null assertion is fragile documentation.
